candidates.stmt:
*About Eva*

I am a committed member of the Cann Hall community and I want to step forward as its next councillor because I genuinely care about the place I call home. I believe local people deserve to be properly heard, and I am driven to ensure decisions reflect the real concerns and priorities of residents — not just a select few.

Professionally, running an NGO that challenges the government on peace and security has strengthened my commitment to fairness, transparency and principled decision-making. My legal background enables me to scrutinise policy and contribute to informed decisions. I understand that national policies often shape local realities, and I believe strong representation means bridging that gap. I want to make a tangible difference in Cann Hall by ensuring residents are genuinely listened to, supported and represented in the decisions that shape their daily lives.

*What Eva stands for*

For me, being Green means working for a better quality of life for everyone in the Ward. Through my lived experience — as a policy and advocacy professional, a young mother and a renter — I know the challenges our community faces, where the Council falls short and how to build power for change. I have supported neighbours with immigration, housing, education and access to healthcare, helping people navigate complex systems and advocate for themselves. Now I want to take that energy into the Council chamber.

I recognise that councillors must represent all residents, even where views differ, and I am committed to listening respectfully, communicating transparently and ensuring everyone understands their rights and options. I am ready to embrace the workload — casework, scrutiny and community engagement — and I will use my voice to stand up for justice, locally and globally. Free Palestine.

**About Tom**

I’m a Product Manager and I live in Leyton, near the Stadium. I’ve spent the last 8 years in education, working with teachers across London and the UK. Schools are a central part of any community and working closely with them has given me a grounding in engaging with difficult problems and a lifelong commitment to the value of listening to people.

Each person is an expert on their own life, and I believe that when governments and officials don’t listen, they tend to make things worse. I’ve been a resident in Waltham Forest for 2 years and I want to start working for the community I live in. I’m standing for the Green Party because for the first time in a long time, I feel hopeful that politics could start to work for normal people again.

**What Tom stands for**

Previously, I’ve campaigned to support young people trying to get back into education after being off-rolled, and for the re-nationalisation of our water companies. Cann Hall deserves a local council that listens, and that puts people ahead of politics. I’ll fight to keep community spaces open and to make housing more affordable.

Too many people are being let down by institutions that should be protecting them. I’ll fight to help residents get the rights and support they’re legally entitled to. I’m running to be a Local Councillor for the Green Party because I want to be one more voice shouting against the flow of money out of communities and into the pockets of the super rich. The odds are stacked against normal people, and the only way to win is if huge numbers of normal people keep on calling for things to be fairer. I want to be one more person fighting for fairness.

*About Peter*

I’ve lived in Cann Hall ward with my family for nearly 15 years – helping me grow roots in a community I’d love to represent as a councillor. Through organising and advocating for local change, I’ve helped establish a residents’ group which successfully campaigned for long-overdue streetscape improvements on Harrow Road, as well as petitioning to help a stalled School Street scheme over the line.

I continue to work with local residents to highlight and address community concerns – but also most importantly as a way of bringing people together. This has shown me what organised, respectful and determined local action can achieve – all experiences I’d be privileged to use as a councillor for Cann Hall.

*What Peter stands for*

My professional background as a designer, researcher and educator means understanding a range of different audiences, and helps shape my   engagement with local issues. I’m interested in how bigger challenges play out in our community – from the gig economy and pollution, to housing failures and the erosion of our public spaces – all experiences that residents raise as local concerns around housing, accessibility and fly-tipping.

As a diligent thinker and doer, I believe we need to create better conversations that reflect how local issues link to wider systems that shape them. I know first-hand the frustration many local residents share around a lack of meaningful representation – too often people feel overlooked and their votes taken for granted. By valuing inclusion, consistency and constructive dialogue, I hope to bring stronger accountability to our local government in Waltham Forest

**About Rachel **
I have lived in Waltham Forest all my life and am very much aware of the local issues. A graduate in Economics and Psychology, a postgraduate in Information Studies and having worked in public libraries for over 30 years, I have a strong interest in supporting education and protecting our local libraries.

I am keen on walking, cycling and sport and am passionate about helping both people and animals. I campaign for animal welfare, volunteer as an animal rescuer for London Wildlife Protection and set up the Waltham Forest Animal Protection group.

**What Rachel Stands For**
"I am excited about the prospect of helping to give a green voice to Waltham Forest. I would like to see the area become safer, with quieter streets, more green spaces, cleaner air and good public transport. It is essential that we protect the planet on behalf of future generations and tackle challenging issues such as pollution and climate change.

The Green Party is committed to a fairer society, providing wellbeing and prosperity to everyone, in harmony with nature.

**About Tom MacInnes**
I’ve lived in the borough for 5 years, having moved around all over London and finally found somewhere to call home. I live with my partner and two small children.

I work for a charity, and have a long standing passion for social justice and environmental issues.

**What Tom stands for**
I want my children to grow up in a happier, greener, fairer borough. I am really energised by the great ideas and passion for change you find in the borough, and want to work with that energy. Incredible things can happen when people work together!

**About Layla Rosa**
I have been a resident of Waltham forest for 16 years, raising my 2 sons and working as an artist and educator. My work in the borough includes; running the Walthamstow Youth Circus (founded in 2014), leading cross sector collaborations across arts, education, well – being and environment to create participatory performance and working with Forest Community land Trust towards sustainable housing and community spaces.

We have witnessed the social, economic and environmental challenges in our time here and recognised the strength of our community and local grass roots organisations coming together to navigate them.

**What Layla Stands For**
I believe in coalition building, that together we are stronger and that social and environmental justice are inextricably linked. I stand now to support the work of coming together to take care of each other and our planet and so the voices of ordinary people are represented in politics.

I will work with the community and grass roots organisations towards greater community cohesion; securing community hubs across our borough that are accessible for all. I will work to create safer streets, opportunities for young people, in support of a fairer food system and to give rights to nature.

**About Sue Wheat**
I have lived in Chapel End for 23 years and always loved the strong and friendly community.

I’ve been involved with various community organisations, including The Hornbeam Centre at Bakers Arms and Friends of Lloyd Park, and I set up Green Drinks to bring people together to talk about green and community issues. I’ve just started helping run People’s Assemblies to discuss local issues and hope to do more.

My new business is ‘Good Life Tours’ visiting inspiring community groups, artists’ studios and green spaces in the borough to bring people together and give people and local projects a boost.

**What Sue Stands For**
I want to help make sure that the community is supported as much as possible in the decision making within the Council chamber, and that vital issues that affect us are acted on, like waste management, making housing developments really affordable and environmentally sound, improving access to GPs, support for our young people and vulnerable people, and much more.

I would hope that my community action and organising, plus a career in media and communications, will help bring the issues that are most important to residents to the fore and create an even better place to live.

**About Luke Glazzard**
I’ve lived in East London for seven years and, with family here all my life, I now firmly call Waltham Forest home. I’ve been campaigning, fundraising and organising since I was a school kid, particularly on issues like housing, equality, migrant rights, and economic & environmental justice.

Professionally, I spent ten years working as a chef, before becoming a mature student and changing careers; I now manage strategic change projects at a publicly funded environmental research organisation, where I work to turn strategy into meaningful outcomes.

From youth campaigning, to running my own business, to international research collaborations, to union organising, I bring strong leadership and a practical, impact focused approach.

**What Luke stands for**
I stand for class-conscious, community-led politics.

Waltham Forest needs strong, visible representation that is prepared to challenge decisions that don’t serve residents. Development should work for the community, not for developers; proper consultation, protection for green spaces, and genuinely affordable housing must be prioritised. We need action on fly-tipping and neglect to ensure a clean, safe neighbourhood we can all take pride in. I want to protect and improve local services, and ensure the safety, inclusion, and wellbeing of all residents, especially those most at risk.

I believe in politics rooted in solidarity, where decisions are made with people, not for them.

**About Michael (Mo) Allen*
Father of three grown up children, all of whom were born in Whipps Cross and educated at Chingford C of E and then Chingford Foundation where I am currently the Chair of Governors.

Living in Chingford Green for over 30 years, residents may know me in earlier guises as the manager of the Waltham Forest Sailing Team for the London Youth Games, as a Diving Coach for Waltham Forest Diving Club or as GSL of the Chingford 32nd Scout Group.

Forest walkers will recognize me as a regular dog walker and runner around Pole Hill, Hawkswood and the Golf Course

**What Mo Stands For**
Like many local parents, expensive housing means I am facing the real prospect that none of my children will be able to stay in London.

Food Security, Energy Security and access to clean water are all important issues that only the Green Party are addressing.

Chingford Green is a special place and I would like to see it remain so. Retaining the Library, the vital 379 and Green Spaces are a priority. Getting a direct bus service to Whipps Cross Hospital and making better use of our Assemby Hall are also achievable ambitions.

**About Kathryn Judd**
Waltham Forest has been my home for 11 years, and for the past 15 I’ve worked as Director of Music in an East London secondary school, driven by a belief that every young person deserves the chance to thrive. I’m proud to lead a supportive, collaborative team where people feel energised and valued.

I also love bringing people together — from street parties and residents’ meetings to a tree preservation action attended by more than 100 neighbours. Most recently, I’ve been working on an air pollution and community health project with Centric Lab, helping residents use data to build confidence, power and a stronger collective voice.

**What Kathryn Stands For**
I care deeply about Waltham Forest — the people, the places, and the communities that make it home — and want stronger local support and genuine engagement with residents. Our borough is vibrant and diverse, yet too many decisions fail to prioritise community wellbeing.

I would work to improve air quality and protect green spaces — from Epping Forest and Chingford Plains to our parks and the trees lining our streets. I want everyone to be able to live and thrive here, with genuinely affordable, energy‑efficient housing shaped with residents. I’m committed to social justice, inclusive services and strengthening local democracy so people feel valued and part of the change.

**About Kris Jack**
Kris lives in Waltham Forest with his husband and two sons. He’s a business owner and entrepreneur who co-founded the UK’s largest mental wellness app for the LGBTQ+ community.

After a PhD in AI, he moved into industry to build things that help real people. He’s co-chaired his school PTA, navigated the SEND system for his boys, and worked with the council to solve problems on his street.

**What Kris Stands For**
I’m standing because people in Chingford Green deserve councillors who actually listen and do the work. Too many people in our communities feel unseen and unsupported.

That’s not good enough.

I believe in working hard, looking out for each other, and helping those who need it. I want more green spaces, stronger communities and housing my children will afford.

**About Jake Smith**
I am a charity worker with experience working for organisations supporting people with cancer, people experiencing homelessness, refugees, and the Armed Forces community.

I first moved to Waltham Forest in 2019 and have since put down firm roots here with my partner. I deeply care about building a fair and just society that works for everyone. I joined the Green Party because they are the only party offering real solutions to the cost-of-living crisis and climate crisis.

**What Jake Stands For**
I am standing for election because I want to make Waltham Forest a fairer, safer, greener place to live. I will work to make the council accountable to local residents and make sure decisions are made for benefit our community.

I will push to make our neighbourhoods a better place to live with a prevention first approach to anti-social behaviour such as fly tipping and greater enforcement for violators.

**About Monika Sobiecki**
I have been a member of the Green Party for over a decade and settled in Waltham Forest just over a year ago. I originally joined the Green Party because they were the only party treating the climate and biodiversity crisis like the emergency that it is.

As a lawyer, I advise people and organisations who have been victims of hacking and surveillance; I also support a non-profit working towards greater corporate accountability and climate justice across different industries.

**What Monika stands for**
The climate crisis will be the defining test of our communities in this century. Local government needs to be a key part of enabling us all to do as much as we can, and supporting people, organisations and businesses to move to low carbon technologies. After we moved to Waltham Forest, we were able to rely on a zero interest loan scheme backed by the Energy Savings Trust to retrofit our home with an air source heat pump, solar panels and a battery system; more residents should be able to do the same, quickly and easily.

Safe, reliable, clean heating and power should be a basic right for everyone in Waltham Forest and subsidised by government. If I am elected, I will work hard to actively explore all options for increasing retrofit and renewables installations in Waltham Forest across every context: public; private; individuals; businesses. Let’s fill Waltham Forest with solar panels!

**About Simone McNichols-Thomas**
I was born in Hackney, but grew up in Oxford – I made my way back to London over 10 years ago and when I moved to Waltham Forest I quickly grew to love its energy and diversity. With a professional background in communications in the Higher Education sector, I care about amplifying important stories and ensuring people have a say in policy change that affects them.

As a second-generation immigrant and someone who belongs to other marginalised communities, I’m passionate about social justice and believe that local government should work for everyone – not just those who already have influence.

If elected, I will work hard to ensure Forest Ward is a place where residents feel heard – regardless of if they voted for me or not.

**What Simone stands for**
I can see divisive politics and austerity measures eroding the foundations of community. That’s why I’m committed to rebuilding forgotten community spaces and supporting grassroots organising.

I’m fortunate to be a recent homeowner and think it’s a basic right to have a secure and affordable home, irrespective of whether you rent or own. I’m also advocating for consistent management of green spaces and accessible, safe walking routes. These should be a right of passage where we live and raise our families.

In my opinion, London is the best city in the world – but I know it can only thrive if it is fair, green and affordable for all.

**About Yuksel Gonul**
I have lived in the borough for over twenty years. I work as a finance coordinator in enviromental management. I bring extensive experience in forecasting, budgeting, and operational efficiency.

My focus is to create cleaner and safer streets in the neighbourhood and improving the rate of recycling to support a greener and fairer future. Beyond my professional role, I am a member of the Kurdish community and a human rights campaigner.

I am commited to addressing local neighbourhood issues and advocating for the well-being of our community.

**What Yuksel Stands For**
I deeply care about our borough and our community and want to contribute to it. I am standing to challenge the current local administration because I want this borough to change for the better. I want to see cleaner and safer streets, and more affordable and social housing.

I also want to see the protection of green environment which is the lifeline of the community. I am for the lower bills, which will help residents who struggles to pay the bills. More importantly I want to ensure the residents’ voices are heard and that they are part of the decision-making process.

I believe in a greener borough and I am looking to bring the change to the area.

**About Paul**

I’m a local resident of ten years, and our community is home in every sense of the word. I’m a parent, a neighbour, volunteer football coach and Co‑Chair of the Waltham Forest Green Party – someone who genuinely cares about where we live.

I’ve worked with communities, in particular young people, for most of my career – but have spent the past 7 years working in London councils. I’ll bring a collaborative and inclusive way of working, a professional background which spans both the charity world and local government, and an understanding of how to bring about change.

I also love cooking and football (until recently coaching the girls’ team at Gwyn Jones) — two things that keep me rooted in the everyday life of the place I call home.

**What Paul stands for**

I’m standing as your Green Party candidate because I believe we deserve councillors who show up, listen, care and follow things through. Instead of waiting for that to happen, I’ve decided to step forward and help bring about that change myself.

My approach is simple and grounded in a few clear values: being present, listening deeply, caring for our community and being honest. I want every voice to be heard. I want people to feel they can reach me. And I want to be clear about what’s possible, while challenging systems that aren’t working for residents.

After many years living in Forest ward, I want to make sure that we all feel part of the decisions that shape our community. Together, we can ensure we live in a place where people feel included, informed and genuinely represented.

**About Sarah Bentley**
Sarah is a Mum and community organiser who’s
lived in the area for 7 years. A former music and social justice journalist turned founder of a community cookery school, during the pandemic she helped provide 200K meals to East Londoners and understands the power of grassroots organising and community participation to bring about positive change.

As someone with ADHD, a teacher of food growing to SEN students, and a mother to a PDA autistic child, she understands the challenges families with additional needs face. She has a track record of hard work, community service and would be an energetic champion for Grove Green.

**What Sarah Stands For**
I am standing as a local councillor as I’d like to play my part in making Waltham Forest a greener, fairer, safer, more thriving borough for all. I am passionate about community participationin politics and feel we need great change across local and national politics to bring everyone in and reimagine a political space where everyone feels welcome and everyone can have their say.

As a resident I am frustrated with how Grove Green and surrounding wards such as Leyton seems to have been left behind by the current council with less care taken to deal with inclusive, safe streets, fly tipping, opportunities for young people, jobs for all and affordable homes.

I’d like to play a part in changing that and would give my all to help solve these challenges in collaboration with the local community.

**About Charlotte Lafferty**
I first moved to Grove Green (Leyton) over 15 years ago and I’m proud to be part of the community here. I’ve been a Unite union rep and have worked for the Green Party for the past seven years in membership growth and engagement.

I’m a queer woman from a working-class background and have spent my adult life renting, so understand first-hand the pressures people face from austerity. Before this, I worked in cleaning, hospitality, customer service and marketing.

I’ve been involved in local campaigns and community organising, with a track record of bringing people together to create meaningful change.

**What Charlotte stands for**
I’m standing to be a strong, independent and Green voice for the community. After years of development that has not properly involved local people, I believe residents must be at the heart of decision-making. I am committed to tackling the housing crisis by prioritising genuinely affordable and social housing over developer interests.

I supports safer streets through community-led, holistic approaches, and I’m dedicated to making Waltham Forest a true Borough of Sanctuary.

I believe climate action must go hand in hand with social justice, ensuring a fairer, greener future that works for everyone.

**About Paul Treacy**
Paul has called Waltham Forest home since 2014 and works in the charity sector, with a background in disability and health advocacy. Growing up working-class, he knows the anxiety of money being tight, and the cost-of-living crisis means that anxiety hasn’t gone away, for him or for many people in our community.

The choice between heating and eating, accumulating debt, the feeling you’re one unexpected bill away from everything falling apart: these are the pressures he wants to address. Volunteering for Faiza Shaheen and Leanne Mohamed showed him what politics should look like (community-led, progressive, and above all, kind) and inspired him to stand in Grove Green.

**What Paul Stands For**
I’m standing for election because Waltham Forest needs a council that makes decisions with the community, not for it. A council that is open, transparent and accountable: one that serves the needs of residents, not political parties or the interests of property developers.

That means tackling the cost-of-living pressures that disproportionately affect the most marginalised in our community, protecting local services, and ensuring every resident has a genuine voice in decisions that affect their lives.

I want to see a Waltham Forest that is a fairer, healthier and greener place to live, and as a councillor, I would work hard to make that happen.

The people of Grove Green deserve a candidate who will listen to them and stand up for them at the council. 

My children either have, or do, attend primary in the school in the ward. I have been involved in organising community events. From being involved in the campaign to save one of the local pubs from becoming flats to stopping a huge mobile phone mast from being built in the middle of a residential area. I have arranged for the school children's work to be displayed in the community. I have helped people with parking permit and blue badge issues.

Previous, Chemical Engineer and Research Scientist for Shell Chemical Co. and later Johnson Matthey/ Universal Oil Products. Senior Science Technician at Highams Park School.
    Dr. William Happer, Professor Emeritus in the Department of Physics at Princeton University is the world authority on CO2 in the atmosphere. Has explained on Youtube “The CO2 Delusion”.
    CO2 is soluble in cold water and less so in warm water. The seas and oceans hold giga tons of CO2, which is a requirement for all life on Earth.
    The current average Ocean surface temperature on Apr. 17, 2026 gives a CO2 level of 430.00 ppm as measured at the Mauna Loa Observatory. Due to various causes, the Oceans are warming and this figure may rise. The measure of CO2 in the atmosphere is proportional to the average temperate of the Oceans and indicates global warming or cooling but does not control it, as UK governments has led us to believe.
    The fuss about CO2 is totally irrational, the Reform Party recognises this, as does the USA. Also, there is dearth of CO2 in the atmosphere, its new level has allowed some farmers to harvest twice a year and is greening desert areas of the planet.
    RESTORING BRITAIN'S POWER & PROSPERITY
    Britain has the richest history of any country in the world, but our best days lie ahead.
    This is a country that built global trade, pioneered industry, defended freedom, and shaped the modern world. Yet for years, the British people have been failed by political leaders who put ideology, bureaucracy, and international institutions ahead of their own citizens.
    The LBWF, after some 30 years of Labour rule is in a mess. The council has closed factories, and built tower blocks and large supermarkets over sites, where we once had fruitful employment. The trains are now packed with commuters traveling to the City for work, before that we had commuters, coming to Highams Park for work.
   The borough is hopelessly in debt, as is the Government. 
Please give me you

Tony has lived in Highams Park for 16 years and works as a teacher, teaching A-level History. In 2014, he was first elected as your local Labour Councillor for Hale End and Highams Park. Tony is originally from North Yorkshire. He was attracted to Highams Park because of its brilliant green spaces, its community feel and proximity to Epping Forest.

Tony was a Governor of Handsworth Primary school between 2008-15. Both his children attended the school and both went on to attend Highams Park School. Tony is also actively involved in the Highams Park Plan, having been elected to its coordinating committee in its first year, and has been enthused by the high level of community involvement in decisions about the future of Highams Park.

If elected, Tony's priorities for Highams Park are:

• Celebrate and conserve our superb green spaces • Reopen The Regal as Highams Park’s cinema • Encourage residents to shop local in Highams Park • Advocate for improved play-spaces for children • Revitalise the Wadham Bridge area • Make our streets safer for women and girls • Make our streets cleaner and expand recycling • Work closely with The Highams Park Planning Group • Improve what’s on offer for young people locally

I grew up in Highams Park and attended Handsworth Primary and Highams Park Secondary schools. I am proud to be Cabinet Member for Culture, Libraries, Sport & Leisure.
After nearly 20 years working as a Stage Manager in theatre, I'm passionate about our borough's culture.
My priorities include restoring the River Ching and creating a thriving cultural hub for Highams Park through the Regal Cinema redevelopment.

**About Brian Capaloff**
For most of my professional life I have worked within different local authorities, both in England and Scotland, mostly in a homelessness role, and am absolutely committed to what councils can do for the more vulnerable people within their communities.

**What Brian stands for**
I have been frustrated by many aspects of the housing/ homelessness work undertaken by the council, whilst being full of admiration for a highly committed, but constrained staff. I am standing with an intention to ensure that the Council fully and effectively enacts its housing responsibilities to those of us in the greatest need.

**About Lukas Simma**
Hi, I’m Lukas. I moved to Waltham Forest after my studies in 2022 to start my career in London, and have since fallen completely in love with this amazing corner of London.

I’m an audio engineer working in the post production industry, and experience the struggles of young professionals in London day to day, from cost of living, high grocery prices, underwhelming quality of housing stock, to crumbling infrastructure and lacking public services.

I hope to bring positive change to Waltham Forest as a Green Party councillor.

**What Lukas stands for**
I joined the Green Party because they were the only party with a real, genuinely new approach to politics.

Not every car park has to be for profit, not every park has to be neglected, not every new housing has to be built for rent only. It’s time to finally stop running the council for profit, and start running it for its residents, regardless of gender, ethnicity, wealth, religion, heritage, sexual orientation, or disability.

What has been lacking in this country was not the knowledge of what to do differently, but the will to do those things differently.

**About Heather Gardiner**

Born in North London, Heather has lived in Waltham Forest for eleven years, the last two in Chingford. Her working life was spent working for local authorities and charitable organisations, both in and outside of London. She spent many years in Housing Management and Homelessness, working in Camden, Hackney and Norwich before becoming a social worker and working in mental health services.

She continues to work part time in social work education. She is a carer, living with an adult son with a severe learning disability who needs full time care. Her two older children live in Waltham Forest.

**What Heather tands for**

We are privileged to live so close to Epping Forest and other green spaces in Chingford. It’s important that we protect these places for the benefit of nature and our mental and physical health. I’m a keen gardener, and a walk leader.

I’m frustrated when people are not listened to or ignored, and have advocated for people's rights. As an unpaid carer, I have personal experience of having to fight for both my son’s and my rights. It shouldn’t be that way.

Community is very important to me: when people are ignored or their needs not met, this can cause division in the community. I want to listen, take action where I can and help bring people together.

**About Rory Fitzgerald**
I have lived in Waltham Forest for 22 years and love green spaces (especially Epping Forest and Walthamstow marshes). I have been active in local politics for many years and care passionately about our borough.

In my work I measure public opinion through surveys in the UK and across Europe and I care passionately about helping people be heard. I hope to bring that passion to my role as a councillor representing everyone in Hatch Lane & Highams Park North.

I am a volunteer for a telephone service for the LGBT community, and I am good at listening to people, a skill often overlooked.

**What Rory stands for**
My priority is to ensure that all areas of the borough are represented in the council, as places like Hatch Lane & Highams Park North often get neglected compared to those closer to Walthamstow. I want to make protecting and improving our green spaces a priority going forward, as too often this gets forgotten. Prioritising a healthy biodiversity for current and future generations is essential.

I also want to see the council do more for those on the lowest incomes who are suffering the most in the cost-of-living crisis. Particularly ensuring that there is access to truly affordable housing.

**About Andrew McMullen**
I have lived in Highams Park for 15 years, before that I grew up in Wanstead so have a strong affinity to Epping Forest. I have worked in corporate sustainability and energy management for the last 20 years and would like to bring some of that experience into the local area.

I have a good understanding of resource management and have managed a number of large projects related to resource efficiency.

I have been a Governor at my children’s school Woodbridge High and I also have supported a number of peope’s assemblies in the London area, looking to improve local community cohesion.

**What Andrew stands for**
Although there are many short term issues we need to address, I am keen to make positive change for our children’s future as well as improving the lives of those in the ward today.

A large part of my job has been to understand how resource shortages will impact the UK and I am keen to ensure our area is as well prepared as possible. One of the best ways to prepare for an uncertain future is to build community cohesion so my biggest commitment as a councilor would be to run regular people’s or citizens’ assemblies. This will help improve our currently fragmented community and will help me deeply understand what people really care about in this ward.

That information will then be what I work toward as a councillor, as I believe that is the best way to be a true voice of the community.

*Socialist Nancy Taaffe to stand in Walthamstow constituency*

Millions will relish the opportunity to register their hatred of the Tories at the ballot box. But Keir Starmer and Stella Creasy’s Labour Party doesn’t fight for the interests of working class people. 

Socialist Party member Nancy Taaffe will stand as part of the Trade Unionist and Socialist Coalition (TUSC).

* Private profit out of our NHS  * Council homes and rent control  * End austerity - inflation-proof pay rises and no more cuts to jobs and public services  * Renationalise rail, mail, water and energy * Stop the onslaught on Gaza  * For a socialist alternative to capitalist crisis, climate destruction and war

For interview: Nancy Taaffe 07786 857673

* Nancy Taaffe won 5,595 votes in the 2024 Greater London Assembly election and 9% of the vote in the Higham Hill council by-election in October 2014  * TUSC did not contest the parliamentary elections when Jeremy Corbyn was leader of the Labour Party, but Nancy was the Socialist candidate in Walthamstow in 2015 and 2010.  * Nancy and TUSC have consistently stood candidates in Waltham Forest council elections, securing 3,490 votes in 2022, 2,841 votes in 2018 and 5,482 votes in 2014. * Nancy Taaffe is Chair of the Save Our Square campaign and Assistant Secretary of Waltham Forest Trades Council. She and the Socialist Party have a long record of fighting for council housing, and against cuts, privatisation and war.

**About Louise Ashcroft**
I’ve lived in Higham Hill 15+ happy years. I love my wonderful neighbours, local nature, arts and all the activities we have on our doorstep. I’m an artist and university lecturer who makes comedy performances and community projects. I work a lot with disabled people and young people, giving them power to advocate for equal rights and bright futures.

I studied green engineering and net zero futures last year, and did a residency in a waste recycling plant. I love running, women’s football (Spurs… sorry!), our allotments, and making imaginative things happen that bring communities together.

**What Louise stands for**
I’d listen to you, neighbours! I’d empower and amplify you.

My values: Clean, safe neighbourhoods. Support creativity and play. Create spaces for social connection and friendship. Support families of all kinds. Protect recreational spaces and nature. Heal the root causes of crime. Safe roads. Champion local businesses. Nurture the new Higham Hill Hub. Affordable housing and access to healthy food are human rights. Divest from war, Free Palestine.

I would work with Labour Councillors effectively, reinforcing green issues like health, wellbeing and equality. I stood for Greens in 2018 and came right behind Labour. Get a green in the mix!

**About Em Dean**
I moved to London in 2016, and I quickly fell in love with this city! I’m a university lecturer in computer science. In politics, I’ve been involved in various policy construction, mostly in education and equalities issues, and some of my policies have become law or official guidance in Wales, Scotland, and overseas.

I also have some expertise in electoral compliance and regulation, and internal party governance. It’s very important to me that political parties work to elect responsible, ethical representatives who put local people first.

**What Em stands for**
I’m standing for the Green Party because I think voters deserve councillors that work for voters, not for themselves or special interests. By electing Greens we are electing a strong and passionate voice for local people and local interests.

**About Marco Hacon**
I’ve lived in London for the past 10 years and have long been connected to Waltham Forest through regular visits to family. Having recently made the area my home, I’m committed to being an active voice for local residents.

I have a Masters’ in Environment, Politics, and development, and have dedicated the last 5 years of my career to tackling climate change.

In my spare time I like to participate in triathlons as well as volunteer for green and social issues.

**What Marco stands for**
I stand for putting people and planet first. That means tackling the climate crisis locally through cleaner air, better green spaces, and sustainable transport. I believe politics should serve communities with decisions shaped by local voices that ensure no one is left behind.

I’m committed to fairness, wellbeing, and practical action that improves everyday life. Ultimately, I’d like to see a more inclusive and resilient Waltham Forest.

I’m Alan Jones, a member of the Higham Hill Focus team and Data Officer for the local party with responsibility for managing local campaign data. Born in East London, I moved to Waltham Forest over 20 years ago and have been living in Walthamstow since 2008. Now retired, I have worked in engineering, service and retail industries.
Having ‘seen the light’ and started to vote Liberal Democrat in a few elections, I joined the party in 2020, and have been campaigning since 2024. I want us to progress in being able to hold national and local government to account with an emphasis on making sure that Waltham Forest has the housing, transport, medical and education services and facilities to support families in making it a prosperous and successful borough.
When not campaigning I volunteer for a homelessness charity. Nationally and internationally I am passionate about moving our country towards electoral reform and actively give support to Ukraine in any way I can.

I’ve lived in Walthamstow for the past five years and am actively involved in the local community through my work with the Liberal Democrats, supporting campaigns that stand up for residents and neighbourhoods.

I joined the Liberal Democrats in 2016 because of the party’s clear stance on Brexit. At the time, I was working freelance in the Czech Republic and was devastated at the prospect of having my EU citizenship, and my right to live and work across Europe, taken away. The Liberal Democrats were the only party prepared to speak up clearly and consistently, and that commitment to openness, fairness and internationalism is what keeps me involved today. I’m currently Vice-Chair of Waltham Forest Liberal Democrats and previously served as Vice-Chair in Islington. While living in Islington, I worked for a homeless charity during the COVID pandemic and I continue to volunteer with them today.

Professionally, I work in film & TV as a sound editor and re-recording mixer. My work demands precision, collaboration and delivering results on time and on budget. These are skills I would bring to holding the Council to account and ensuring residents’ money is spent responsibly.

As a councillor, my priorities would reflect the Liberal Democrats’ “Six to Fix”: protecting local health services, tackling the housing crisis, making our streets safer, fixing roads and pavements, defending our environment, and improving waste and recycling services. I’m committed to being an approachable, hardworking councillor who listens and delivers for our community.

**About Laura Pearce**
I moved to Walthamstow five years ago, and love the Borough. I grew up in a working class household in Somerset. The first person in my family to go to university, where I studied law. I am now a solicitor working for a trade union, a job I love: getting to advocate for people’s rights and influence Government policy.

**What Laura stands for**
I believe in communities and working together to make a better life in the Borough. I want to use my skills to influence positive changes for everyone in Waltham Forest. By listening to what residents want, I hope I can ensure the needs of our community are met.

**About Ijaz Kato**
Walthamstow for me has always been my anchor for who I am. Having been born in Whipps Cross, I’ve lived in D.C., Singapore and Bristol before coming back permanently to Walthamstow and have lived here for 5 years. I’ve always travelled back to Walthamstow as I’ve seen it as my home, and want to do what I feel it’s done for me for most of my life, make Walthamstow a home for everyone.

I work in the Film and TV industry as a Production Co-ordinator and run my own film distribution company! I also help volunteer at Rukshana Khan Foundation.

**What Ijaz stands for**
For me, the biggest issues are in relation to accessibility, in terms of disabled access along with accessibility to services like garbage cleaning. Creating green spaces, clearer air and effictive services for those who need it.

Flytipping especially is an issue that I feel needs to be solved to make the streets a more welcoming and habitable place. Along with this, as a filmmaker, providing artistic opportunities for young people can provide a great outlet not currently being provided to children.

**About Hannaan Malik**
Hannaan is a proud Walthamstow local, born and raised here, and now standing as the Green Party candidate for Hoe Street ward. Having grown up in the area and attended local schools, he understands first-hand the pressures residents face, from the cost of living to housing, cleaner streets and safer neighbourhoods.

As a foster carer, Hannaan has spent years supporting vulnerable young people and families, offering stability, care and practical help when it is needed most. This experience has shaped his belief that strong communities are built on compassion, fairness and looking out for one another.

**What Hannaan stands for**
Hannaan is standing to give Hoe Street a fresh, community-focused voice, one that listens, acts and puts residents first. He is committed to making the area greener, safer and more affordable, while protecting the sense of community that makes Walthamstow such a special place to live.

**Sarah Chaney** brings over a decade of experience campaigning for better housing and tenant rights. Living on a newbuild estate, she has held housing associations and developers to account, fighting for repairs, preventing unfair evictions and securing compensation for neighbours. With a background in arts, education and health union work, Sarah has also helped establish youth provision locally and supported a wide range of community wellbeing activities. She knows there’s appetite for change in South Chingford, and wants to help our community realise that something different is possible. She is standing to deliver better housing, stronger services and a more resident-led approach to local politics.

She is standing alongside Deborah Hollingsworth and Jennifer Larbie as Chingford & Woodford Green Community Independents.

**Vote Independent in the local elections on Thursday 7 May and we pledge to**

1. Ensure the community has a say. For too long, the needs of South Chingford have been ignored, and many of us locally feel that we have no voice. We pledge to hold People’s Assemblies for South Chingford, working with local community associations and the voluntary sector to support and empower the volunteers and community groups that serve our area and bring the community together.

2. Develop a community plan for South Chingford. Our area is poorly served for community services and public transport, and our town centre is run down. We will work with local community groups to create a South Chingford Area Plan that reaches beyond Chingford Mount, considering the needs of everyone living here.

3. Hold developers to account. South Chingford needs more housing, but it also needs development that invests in the local community. We pledge to hold developers to account on delivering quality local homes, including social housing, alongside the amenities that will improve our area.

4. Develop a climate resilience plan. Flooding, overheating and extreme temperatures which p

_I am standing to ensure local decision-making works for everyone — especially the most vulnerable._
I have lived in the Highams Park and South Chingford area for over 15 years and am deeply embedded in local community life. A teacher for more than 20 years, I have held senior leadership roles including Assistant Headteacher and SENDCo. Now an Advisory Teacher for a local authority, I am a a passionate advocate for children with additional needs and for inclusive education. 
I am standing alongside Jennifer Larbie and Sarah Chaney as Chingford & Woodford Green Community Independents. Our group grew out of the 2024 General Election, when the national Labour Party deselected our popular local candidate Faiza Shaheen and parachuted in a candidate with no connection to the area. Then, as now, growing frustration with both the Labour and Conservative parties has led to a total lack of genuine local representation.

We will work with local community groups to create a South Chingford Area Plan that reaches beyond Chingford Mount, considering the needs of everyone living here.

_I am standing as an independent to serve the community - not a political party._
I bring extensive experience in trade union organising, policy development and campaigning and I currently lead advocacy, policy and campaigns at Christian Aid, working to secure justice for the world’s most marginalised people.
A local resident, mum and member of St Edmund’s Church, I am standing alongside Deborah Hollingsworth and Sarah Chaney as Chingford & Woodford Green Community Independents. Our group grew out of the 2024 General Election, when the national Labour Party deselected our popular local candidate Faiza Shaheen and parachuted in a candidate with no connection to the area. Then, as now, growing frustration with both the Labour and Conservative parties has led to a total lack of genuine local representation.
