The UK must return to full EU membership. In the meantime, the best possible relationships with fellow EU democracies must be promoted and maintained.

*About Liz*

I’ve lived in Lea Bridge for more than a decade and I love being part of the vibrant, diverse and welcoming community here. I enjoy being active in the local area; my community work includes scrutinising development plans, organising community forums & street parties and successfully pressuring the council to tackle illegal car meets nearby.

I am a documentary filmmaker, a profession that is based on giving people a voice. It has equipped me with the skills essential in a local councillor, including the ability to research any given topic and communicate it effectively to varied audiences. I collaborate with interested parties from all walks of life, whilst producing tangible results on time and within budget.

*What Liz stands for*

Lea Bridge is one of the most deprived wards in the borough, and it feels like an afterthought to our current council. We face unprecedented change here, including thousands of new homes built on flood plains and dangerously contaminated land. We need representation from people who care about this community and have the skills and passion to stand up for it.

The homes built here must be suitable for long-term living and affordable for everyone. We must not allow the council to continue breaking their commitments to us – where is our promised health centre? Why are they building on our parks and removing the greenbelt protections of our land?

Lea Bridge is my home – and I pledge to represent my community in the council.

**About Solene**

I have lived and worked in East London for over 17 years and have long been vocal in campaigning with and on behalf of local resident groups. I am not a career politician, but a local and outspoken resident who has grown increasingly frustrated with party politicians who fail to attend community meetings, respond to emails or properly represent the people they were elected to serve. I believe I can do a better job of addressing residents’ concerns than the current Labour councillors, whom many feel are largely absent.

I care deeply about my neighbourhood and the diverse community that calls it home. I see serious issues being overlooked due to weak representation: a lack of meaningful engagement, overstretched GP services, large private-sector tower blocks, neglect of nature and green spaces, dangerously high air pollution, unsafe playgrounds and a shortage of spaces for young people. At the same time, the council continues to approve major unaffordable developments that will dramatically increase the ward’s population, without strengthening the already strained public services serving Lea Bridge.

**What Solene stands for**

Residents are being ignored.

Parents don’t even try calling the GP anymore — it’s overwhelmed and impossible to reach — so they’re forced to take their children straight to A&E.

Neighbours are worried about more than 10 tower blocks being built on contaminated Gasworks land, over six years and within the same half mile. Neighbouring 3 schools including 2 nurseries and no guarantees on monitoring air quality and toxic dust on and in homes.

Our green spaces are essential. They protect our health, preserve wildlife, clean polluted air, absorb heavy rainfall and help safeguard us on a flood plain. Building over them puts our future at risk.

The council appears quicker to cooperate with developers than to stand up for residents’ interests.

Poor transport planning is damaging daily life. We’ve lost a key bus route, there’s no

**About Rosie**

I am a Londoner born and bred, and I have lived in the Lea Bridge ward for over a decade. Raising my family here, I understand the importance of community and I am increasingly aware of the impact local government decisions have on our day-to-day lives. I have worked in the entertainment industry for 20 years as a circus artist, performing around the globe and teaching performing arts to adults and young people of all ages.

I love the joy and connection that live performance can bring, and the expansive way in which circus pushes the boundaries of what is humanly possible. I believe we need this same approach in our politics, which is why I join the Greens in 2021. Too often ordinary people are left feeling powerless against big business and monied interests, without a real say in decision-making. But when we come together, our power is limitless.

**What Rosie stands for**

Having been encouraged to stand as a councillor by fellow Waltham Forest Green Party members, I am keen to meet other Lea Bridge residents and hear their concerns. I aim to promote the needs of ward residents as strongly as possible. Lea Bridge is one of the most deprived areas of Waltham Forest, with the lowest number of GP surgeries relative to its population.

It is vulnerable to overdevelopment, and multiple projects in the pipeline will put the community’s health at risk. I hope to scrutinise all plans affecting Lea Bridge residents and advocate for my community. I am also outraged by the inaction our current councillors have shown in relation to the war crimes being committed by the state of Israel with the help of the UK government. I promise to push the council to fulfil its commitment to divest pension funds from all companies complicit in war crimes.

**About Daisy Richards**
Daisy has had a lifelong connection to Waltham Forest,b having been born in Whipps Cross and now living in Leyton. After a rewarding eigth year teaching career, she now works as a therapist; helping people navigate mental health challenges with empathy and practical support.

Daisy understands the link between living standards and wellbeing. She is standing to implement community-centred changes in Leyton. Daisy is a member of Waltham Forest for a Free Palestine and the Waltham Forest Community Coalition.

**What Daisy stands for**
I am standing in Leyton because I genuinely care about our residents; my neighbours. We deserve a bigger say in how our ward is run, with decisions aligned with community values and priorities.

I want Leyton to be a place where people from all different backgrounds feel safe, supported and welcome, whilst ensuring the basic needs of every resident is met. It is essential that lifelong residents of Waltham Forest are able to remain in the borough and therefore ensuring housing is affordable is crucial.

I will amplify the voices of our community, to create the future we deserve.

**About Rob Gardner**
Rob lives and works in Leyton, helping run his family’s long-established business. He’s been involved in community organising that supports small and independent businesses across East London, and has helped run divestment campaigns to prevent universities investing in fossil fuels and the arms trade.

**What Rob stands for**
I’m standing for council because Leyton deserves better representation. Decisions are often made without properly listening to residents or supporting local businesses. I want to change that by being visible, accountable, and focused on the issues people actually care about. I’m keen to make sure the community is properly represented in the council and ensure Greens bring about transformative change based on their values.

**About Demetrius Williams**
I’m standing to be a Green Party councillor because our borough deserves councillors who are visible, honest and focused on what matters to residents.

As a local campaigner and Waltham Forest resident, I’ve worked with a number of local community groups that amplify underrepresented voices in the borough. This includes Waltham Forest for a Free Palestine, which is currently working to hold the council accountable to its pledge to divest its pension investments from companies complicit or linked to war crimes.

**What Demetrius stands for**
My priorities include:

_Housing and Green Spaces_
Push for genuinely affordable, high-quality homes that meet environmental standards and hold private developers to account. Protect our green spaces and preserve their biodiversity and community life.

_Health and Social Care_
Promote accessible, quality healthcare (especially for vulnerable residents) and strengthen local social care support.

_Safer, Cleaner Streets_
Tackle pollution, improve safety and keep our streets and public spaces clean, inclusive and well-maintained.

_Fair Local Economy_
Back small businesses and community groups that ensure support reaches those who need it most.

_Community Solidarity and Ethics_
Stand up for fairness, equality and environmental justice to ensure our borough acts with compassion.

I’ll put our community first. I’ll challenge complacency and lead with the energy, compassion and empathy needed to serve Leyton.

I am standing for election in Leyton because working-class families in our community are being pushed to the breaking point. We are being priced out of our own neighbourhoods by skyrocketing, unaffordable housing, squeezed by low pay and a lack of secure work, and abandoned by devastating local cuts to the essential council services we rely on every day. It is time to fight back against austerity and demand a council that puts the needs of ordinary people before profit.

​Cut Rents

Restore Jobs

Restore Services

Low pay and the need to defend our union rep left
bus drivers at Lea Interchange with no choice but to go on strike. We had fantastic support from passengers and from socialists and trade unionists in Waltham Forest, and we won!

If we can organise against bad bosses, we can organise against councils trying to implement cuts, and we can bring together people to fight for the funding we need. If I get elected I will bring my experience of organising in the workplace to organising in our communities to fight against council cuts, for jobs, homes and services for all
• **CUT RENTS**
• **CREATE JOBS**
• **RESTORE SERVICES**

**VOTE AGAINST AUSTERITY, WAR AND DIVISION**

**About RoseMary Warrington**
Has lived in Leytonstone since 1985, joined the Green Party in 2009, is a retired IT Business Analyst, on the committees of Bushwood Residents Association, News from Nowhere Club, and Transition Leytonstone where I help run the Community Fridge.

**What RoseMary stands for**
I want the people of Leytonstone to thrive in a sustainable and strong community that works with the natural world, now and for future generations.

As your councillor I will be accountable and open, listening to your needs and concerns and working to provide the best possible service in a constrained and uncertain world.

I was born in Whipps Cross and grew up in the area. I love living in Leytonstone, and that is mainly down to the people who live here. The community is so active in supporting others, and in making it a creative and engaging place to live in. I founded Leytonstone Folk; a monthly community music group, and I am involved in a number of local events and community groups. I feel really lucky to live here and would feel so honoured if elected.

I have been a teacher for 17 years, and have worked across mainstream, SEN and alternative provisions. I currently oversee provision for children who have barriers to education. I am a qualified Special Needs Coordinator and have been a Governor at a number of schools. I have learnt so much in my career that I am so grateful for. However, I have seen the number of barriers increase for families who are looking to get the right support for their children, and teachers becoming more stretched each year. We all deserve better and this is something I am really passionate about creating positive change for.

Prior to last year, I had rented in London for 18 years, so I know how challenging the housing market is, and how unbalanced and unfair it is. Rent increases are also impacting local businesses, and we really need to be putting pressure on the government to implement rent and business rate controls/decreases.

The environment and effective climate action is an area that needs a lot more focus. There are fantastic local initiatives around sustainability and collective food growing in the borough, and I would love to support this further and create more opportunities for growing, alongside other sustainability initiatives.

I value kindness and honesty, and making sure that everyone has access to the services they need. I want to listen, to help raise other’s voices, and I want to see more collaboration around decision making.

**About Chanté Johnson**
My name is Chante, I am a daughter, sister, very proud auntie and loyal friend, London has been a constant in my life and my love for its ecosystem has only grown, the ability to find community no matter who you are or what you’re looking for, you can find it here.

I have worked service jobs to remote jobs making me no stranger to hard work. Politics has been another constant as a young black woman it seems intrinsically linked to my identity leading me to write my university dissertation on black hair and all that it means. Seeking out opportunities such as volunteering for organisations such as our streets now, even going vegan.

I am always looking for ways to better, make a difference and most importantly to help.

**What Chanté stands for**
I am standing for Markhouse because this is how we make the positive change we desperately need. It’s normal everyday people stepping forward and stepping up to help the local community in whichever way we can. I want to speak up for people who no longer feel heard, to put the needs of my community first. I can use my knowledge and capabilities to fight for what is right and what is fair, to challenge and change the status quo that keeps our cost of living far too high.

I have (as I suspect we all have) watched from the sidelines and felt the hopelessness at watching our country go down a path of decisiveness and what seems like never ending crisis. So, I am saying let’s all step forward and step up and choose hope, lets elect Greens! and create the change we want to see.

**About Richard Wild**
Hi, I’m Richard. I was born in London and have been a resident of Markhouse now for over 20 years. I’m a criminologist and university lecturer but have always also been a human rights and trade union activist. I started a street group during lock down and do what I can supporting various local community and environmental groups.

It made sense to step up and see what I could do as a member of the Green Party in Waltham Forest but most importantly try to bring change and hope for my friends and neighbours in what are increasingly dark times.

**What Richard stands for**
Please vote for me as your councillor for Markhouse so that I can fight for you, for a safer, cleaner, greener and fairer Waltham Forest.

I think we can be much more ambitious together – improving air quality, public transport, affordable housing, and insulating to bring down bills. We need to be more radical and pro-social in our solutions in ways that supports everyone, are inclusive, prioritise the well-being of young people, the vulnerable and safeguards the future.

I would always seek to do this with the community, in a transparent and honest way, answerable to you.

* Standing to represent Special Educational Needs children and adults.

* Standing against privatisation of NHS, Schools.

* Standing against council tax and council rent increases.

* Supporting no cuts budget.

* Supporting 15 minutes free parking, and lenient parking restrictions ending early in evening to revive high streets.

* Supporting low business rates.

* Supporting transparent house allocation policies.

I'm not from here. I grew up in Aotearoa New Zealand, and was drawn to make St James my home because of its long history of artisans and makers. I own a small business, where I use my creative practice to imagine better futures.

I’ve used creativity to redesign parks, recruit teachers and help women advocate for better outcomes.

Having volunteered with care experienced young people for many years, I know a well run council could change everything for our young people. Our ward has a once in a lifetime chance to vote for hope, and I want to be part of that.

Labour has taken St James for granted and the evidence is on our streets: rising costs, dying trees, and young people with nowhere to go and nothing to do. We can’t afford a council that doesn’t work for us.

I want public money used to build community wealth for the long term. I want the council to invest proactively in the next generation rather than reacting when things go wrong.

And I want residents in the room when decisions get made about our futures, not told about them afterwards.

*About David*

I live in St James Ward, my son goes to school here. My family are from Waltham Forest and my mum was a nurse at Whipps Cross. I work in the NHS as an art psychotherapist for CAMHS (Child and Adolescent Mental Health Services).

As a Unite rep I’ve doubled union membership in my workplace and been elected to the London and Eastern Regional Industrial Committee for Health.

I’ve been organising in this community for years, inside and outside of party politics. I’m standing as a councillor because it’s clear that only Labour or Green can win here, and this ward deserves better than what Labour has delivered.

*What David stands for*

St James is full of people already doing the work to make this a great place to live. What we need is a council that backs them rather than bypasses them.

I want to open up how decisions get made here, through genuine planning transparency, citizen assemblies and community-led development. On housing, that means exploring new methods of ownership for homes that are actually affordable.

For nature, that means council services that protect the green spaces we all enjoy. I’ve spent my career helping communities build from the ground up, and I’m ready to bring that to the council chamber.

*About Martin*

I’ve lived in Waltham Forest since 2022, and grew up in Newham. I’m an NHS GP, so every day I see how poor housing, inequality and lack of access to care affect people’s health directly. I’m working hard to fix this.

I’ve led a cardiovascular outreach programme across Waltham Forest, and during the pandemic I protected vulnerable communities. I’ve heard many of our neighbours share a concern about inadequate GP provisions.

Only a loud Green voice on this council can advocate for fair care access.

*What Martin stands for*

I want to get the basics right: GP services, planning decisions made openly, and public money used to build long-term resilience rather than short-term profit. Councillors should answer to us residents, that’s the job.

The demolition of medical practices without adequate replacement is exactly what happens when developers aren’t held to account.

As a GP I understand the importance that strong health infrastructure and quality housing has on our wellbeing, and will be advocating for these on behalf of our community.

I’ve lived in Walthamstow for 13 years, and many of you will know me from conversations on your doorstep. St James is a special place to me, and over the last four years as one of your councillors I’ve supported more than 1,000 local residents with housing problems, anti-social behaviour and other issues - a strong track record of getting things done for local residents.

I care deeply about this community and about making sure no one is left behind.

I’m not a career politician. I’ve worked since I was 15, and a Labour government changed my life. I’m now the CEO of a women’s centre and have spent over 20 years working in healthcare and supporting women and girls.

As your councillor, I’ve led work across the borough on health inequalities, initiated Waltham Forest becoming a Borough of Sanctuary, and supported declaring a nature emergency - putting those values into action locally.

Locally, I’ve helped secure the new St James medical centre and fought to save CRATE, protecting local businesses and jobs. I’ve also supported investment in our parks and green spaces, backed nature recovery and worked to create opportunities for local people. I’m Chair of Governors at a local primary school.

I’ll keep working hard for St James and standing up for local residents - and it would be an honour to have your support.

I have lived in Walthamstow for 20 years. I have actively helped build the incredible community spirit we have with an extensive list of volunteering locally including: volunteering at Eat or Heat our local food bank, co-founding Walthamstows first food pantry and refurbishing a building for a community living room that also provided early support to residents in need. I also co-founded Queens Boundary Community a resident’s group based in Markhouse ward.

Being part of protecting and enhancing our environment is important to me. I lead a local re-wilding group and together have greened Walthamstow Cemetery, a local park and rain gardens. Our cemetery meadow now hosts hedgehogs, bats, red list birds as well as rarer bees and butterflies.

I have organised a range of community cohesion events including park events, street parties, community iftars, exercise classes for women and children as well as commissioning Project Zero to support young adults in our local park.

I also chair my local Safer Neighbourhood Panel and volunteer for a local animal rescue  charity.

My career has been focussed within the charity and Local Government sector: two key areas of focus leading projects tackling climate change and more recently providing homes for Grenfell survivors.

I am passionate about Councils and the positive outcomes that can be achieved when we get it right: enhancing outcomes for our residents and improving our environment. I have the knowledge and experience that will enable me to get to work right away and my neighbours next door in Markhouse Ward can confirm that I will work hard and deliver!

I was born and raised in Walthamstow. was educated at Kelmscott School on Markhouse Road, learned to kick a football and ride a bike over St James Park and have lived at both ends of the ward. I work as a trade union organiser, fighting for social and economic justice for some of London’s most precariously employed and vulnerable public sector workers.

Growing up in our bit of Walthamstow has shaped my values and made me the person I am today, and it would be an incredible privilege to have the chance to represent you as your councillor and bring those values to Waltham Forest Town Hall.

I'm proud of the Waltham Forest Labour's record in our community:
- Regularly putting the borough in the top 5 for council house builders in the country (this is particularly important to me as a young person who's grown up in the borough and seen so many of my friends struggle with the choice between staying with their community or moving somewhere affordable)
- Delivering a brand new health centre for our community at Jazz Yard near St James Street
- Protecting and supporting local businesses, for example stepping in to keep CRATE St James open
- Proactively working with the police and other partners to tackle crime and antisocial behaviour in our community, whether that's knife crime affecting our young people, phone snatching at Blackhorse Road and St James stations or parcel theft across our ward

I promise if I'm elected in May, I will work tirelessly to serve and support our community.

Caramel lives in the heart of Upper Walthamstow and has lived in Walthamstow for 25 years. She is a writer, a parent, walks her dog here and has an allotment here. She hosts a Mini Food Bank and a Little Free Library, have collected donations for DEC Ukraine appeal, refugees in Lesvos and Calais, and local homeless teams. She chairs a residents’ association and pulled together local Covid-19 volunteers at the beginning of the pandemic, before the government responded. Caramel already works extensively in our community, and as your Councillor she will do even more for people.

**About Rbeeza Mobeen**
My name is Rbeeza, and I’ve lived in Walthamstow since primary school, so a lot of my formative upbringing was around these parts!

I’m now a social impact reporter for a fabulous little charity that combines art and activism to bring about social change, and love bringing my artsy expertise and lived experience as a first-gen migrant to our workshops and projects. You’ll probably find me at the libraries checking out a gazillion books (or asking for info on how to book the space for an event) or at the wetlands, doing a walk and talk with local charities.

I’m always looking to uplift the most marginalised voices in our community, and learning from those who came before me in the fights for justice.

**What Rbeeza stands for**
As a first-generation immigrant, I’ve seen what making non-abolitionist local reforms does to strengthen the existing social inequality in an area.

I stand for the populations residing in Waltham Forest: our communities, our livelihoods, and our chances at succeeding in life should be made easier and more accessible, rather than prioritising vacant luxury flats that none of us can afford.

I stand for the families in Waltham Forest whose politics are unheard and cast aside, and who feel that they’re being pushed to the sidelines as a result of ever-increasing socio-economic policies.

A Chingford resident and higher education professional, Elizabeth is currently the only Labour Councillor in Chingford. Since being elected four years ago, Elizabeth has worked hard to support residents with thousands of issues and enquiries, and built a strong relationship with the local community in South Chingford as well as with local businesses. From securing investment in children’s play areas, to making our streets safer, Elizabeth has achieved results as a local Councillor. There’s more work to do and Valley needs a Labour team who will work with Elizabeth to get the very best deal for Valley.

Elizabeth's priorities for Valley are: • Make our streets cleaner and expand recycling • Conserve and expand our superb green spaces • Secure a new youth hub for young people in Chingford • Ramp up enforcement against fly-tippers • Make our streets safer and tackle speeding • Support Chingford Mount ‘s local economy to thrive • Continue to improve local play-spaces for children • Campaign for better transport links for South Chingford and Chingford Mount

As a new father and local resident, my priority is building a community where every family can thrive in a safe, supportive, and inclusive environment. I am standing as an Independent to ensure our area is driven by our residents’ needs, rather than Westminster party lines. As a Fellow of the ACCA with an MBA, I offer the professional expertise and motivation to provide the rigorous oversight needed to protect our vital services. I am motivated by a sense of duty to ensure our ward is managed with integrity and a true focus on the needs of our community.

For much of my life I remained apolitical, but that changed as I witnessed a growing culture of mismanagement and a political landscape that too often relies on gaslighting and self-interest. This disillusionment deepened with the conflict in Gaza, which exposed a painful gap between the values of compassion we claim to hold as a nation and the reality of our political actions, which are now too often focused on division and empty slogans.

As a new father, I recognise that the foundations of my child’s future begin in our local community, in the very streets and services we use every day. I want Chingford to be an environment where everyone can thrive - a place defined by safety, opportunity, and a genuine sense of family and belonging. I have realised that we cannot simply wish for a better community; we have to be the ones to help build it. Driven by these values, I am standing for council to champion a vision centred on a true conscience, one that strives for an equitable society where everyone is treated with dignity, regardless of their background.

I have spent my professional life building expertise in financial and strategic matters, having earned my Fellowship with the Association of Chartered Certified Accountants (ACCA) and completed my Master’s in Business Administration (MBA). This professional background, combined with my experience in community governance as a Chair and Treasurer of local clubs and

*If I am elected as a councillor, I would work to strengthen community connections and ensure residents’ priorities are clearly represented.*
I've lived locally for almost 10 years and I work in higher education supporting student wellbeing. I am a  lifelong trade unionist and am standing as an independent to promote community-led politics and tackle growing inequality. I support the development of local People’s Assemblies to give residents a direct voice in shaping the future of South Chingford and the wider area. 
I am one of three *Chingford & Woodford Green Community Independents* standing, alongside *Saqeeb Howlader and Matt Romo.*
*Vote Independent in the local elections on Thursday 7 May and we pledge to:*
*Ensure the community has a say.* For too long, the needs of South Chingford have been ignored, and many of us locally feel that we have no voice. We pledge to hold People’s Assemblies for South Chingford, working with local community associations and the voluntary sector to support and empower the volunteers and community groups that serve our area and bring the community together.
*Develop a community plan for South Chingford.* Our area is poorly served for community services and public transport, and our town centre is run down. We will work with local community groups to create a South Chingford Area Plan that reaches beyond Chingford Mount, considering the needs of everyone living here.
*Hold developers to account.* South Chingford needs more housing, but it also needs development that invests in the local community. We pledge to hold developers to account on delivering quality local homes, including social housing, alongside the amenities that will improve our area.
*Develop a climate resilience plan.* Flooding, overheating and extreme temperatures which put our homes and families at risk are associated with the climate emergency. We pledge to embed climate resilience in everything we do, protecting our green spaces like Larks Wood, developing flood

Born at Whipps Cross, I am a lifelong Chingford resident with 8 years experience working in the NHS and social care, instilling in me a sense of service to local people.

I know the area and will put residents’ voices at the heart of everything if elected, particularly those more in need.

Most recently I’ve worked in occupational therapy with older adults and disabled people in the community, assisting with assessments for home adaptations. Before that, in nursing, with adolescents in mental health crisis.

My first job in the NHS was in safeguarding, where I compiled information to track perpetrators of abuse and worked across agencies for the safety of residents. My first ever job was working at Sports Direct in Chingford for £5 an hour at 16.

In my new role working for NELFT in Waltham Forest, I am amplifying patient and carer voices, coproducing change and improvement to services, alongside those that use them and their families.

I’m proud to stand here, having competed with Chingford Rugby Club for 6 years and with Waltham Forest Gators in swimming.

A strong sense of community started out in the Scout Association, where I spent 11 years of my youth and volunteered frequently at Gilwell Park, running activities for young people.

I also volunteer at Hawkwood Nature Reserve, keeping local green spaces tidy where I grew up and volunteered at Highams Hub foodbank during COVID lockdown.

**About Ben Aaron Copsey**
Hi! I’m Ben, one of your Green Candidates for William Morris Ward. I’ve been living in Waltham Forest for nearly ten years, and have been active in the community for environment and people-first developments since day one in the borough!

I work in Access to Higher Education and am proud to say I’ve spent my professional life so far working towards educational equity for London’s amazing young people.

I know what we can do, together, when we put the effort in to listen, collaborate and put health, happiness and environment above austerity and developer profit margins.

**What Ben stands for**
As a Councillor I will stand very simply for the hope – and determination – that life for everyone in the borough can get better.

We deserve better air quality, protected green space and open land, investment in the areas of the borough that really need the help, and developments that work for the communities they’re in, instead of lining the pockets of developers at the cost of our health.

I will work for resident-led policy and democracy, clean air, green space, education, opportunities and enjoyment for our young people, and social justice at every level of government.

**About Robyn Page**
I’m a proud Walthamstowaway, I’ve lived here since 2019. You’ll often find me queueing for a seeded loaf at Wild Grains bakery, enjoying a pint of Skoosh at Exale, or cycling down to the marshes on my bright red bike.

I’m a systems thinker, which makes me good at seeing the big picture. I’ve been using this skill for nearly two decades to solve complex problems in the arts industry. I’d love to use it to improve the lives of everyone in Waltham Forest.

I’m a knitter, a drummer, a cat lover, and I’m one of your Green candidates for council.

**What Robyn stands for**
I joined the Green Party as a student, because they were the only political party who understood that climate, social, and economic justice must go hand-in-hand. Sadly I think they’re still the only one.

Waltham Forest needs and deserves Green representation on the council. We have a real opportunity here to make our council much more democratic and accountable, and empower residents to have a hand in what happens in their community.

I believe we all have a right to quality, affordable housing; clean air and access to nature; education and opportunity for growth. I stand with marginalised people and against oppression.

**About Peter Ibrahim Kanyike**
I’m proud to have been born in Whipps, raised in Walthamstow, and went to school in Leyton and Leytonstone. My career is in health policy & strategy, systematically tackling inequalities and listening & working with community groups on their lived experiences.

Being a councillor would be an extension of my work serving and helping people to improve their lives.

I’m a revert to Islam for over 3 years, so you will find me in my local mosque or supporting the revert centre in Leyton. Otherwise, you will see me in the Gym in the Market or with my old schoolmates playing football in Goals Chingford every Sunday night!

**What Peter stands for**
As my career shows, I care about improving lives, especially those in need and the vulnerable. We have all been let down in our living standards for too long, yet I believe in hope and the possibility of change for good.

I found the Green Party as the place to continue this mission to help people across society, including those oppressed around the world through genocide and human rights abuses. From health services, to climate, to the local economy, to infrastructure & housing and beyond, I will work for a future where all people can contribute and are integrated into the decisions that impact their lives.

We will be a shining example to the rest of the country and I will deliver with honesty, transparency, accessibility and accountability.

l've lived in Walthamstow since 1982 and in Wood Street since 1994. Before becoming a councillor, / worked as a professional singer and social worker, and served as a school governor and on the Equity union's governing body. I've been proud to represent Wood Street since 2002, standing up for our community and protecting the services people rely on.

My priorities:

1. Tackling ASB
2. Wood Street Station
3. Help small businesses

I've lived in Walthamstow for over a decade and volunteered in my communities since 2010. / work in child protection research, and I'm passionate about supporting Wood Street residents and helping every family feel safe and supported in our community.

My priorities:

1. Tackling ASB
2. Wood Street Station
3. Help small businesses

**About Charlie Loveday**
Hi! I’m Charlie. I live in Wood Street and love our community here. The creativity and diversity of talent that we have both in Wood Street and the wider borough never ceases to amaze me. It’s something the council could do so much more to unlock.

I work in community involvement, and have spent my working life across local government and the NHS helping organisations meaningfully involve local people in the decisions and planning processes that shape their lives. I’m standing not to speak on behalf of residents, but to make sure the council genuinely has to listen to them.

**What Charlie stands for**
I want to be a councillor who sees their job not as managing the decline of the borough, but as building the power of the people in it.

Local democracy can be more than a consultation letter that arrives after the decision is made. It can mean residents shaping proposals from the start. Budgets opened up for real scrutiny. Developers held to the promises they make. These aren’t technical decisions that require expert answers, they require lived experience.

Wood Street is full of people who know what needs to change. What’s been missing is a council willing to hear it.

**About Samir Richards**
Delighted to be an adopted member of the Walthamstow Community. I moved to Walthamstow from Luton in 2022 and have loved every second living here. I am an Assistant Principal at a high performing school locally.

I have immersed myself into the borough and you may have seen me at Walthamstow FC (or Arsenal), cycling along the canal or spending an afternoon at any of the many breweries, though the Flower Pot remains the favourite. I have two (black and white and very unusual looking for a labradoodle) labradoodles that I walk regularly at Hollow Pond and Lloyd Park on the weekend.

**What Samir stands for**
Social justice – I’ve worked in schools for nearly 10 years now and I’ve seen the difference we can make on a daily basis. I hope that working in this capacity means I can do more to support even more people.

Local councils affect people’s lives on a day-to-day basis and I think we can do better. I want a greener, more affordable Walthamstow that benefits everyone.

Born and raised in the borough, Alex has called Wood St home for 20+ years. Alex works in widening participation, supporting students from underrepresented backgrounds to access higher education and future career opportunities. He also serves as a secondary school governor who has firsthand experience working with young people from a range of backgrounds. He volunteers locally for the likes of Salaam Peace and Wood St First and is connected to a range of local social justice groups. Alex supports investment in accessible public spaces and more social housing, and wants to serve Wood St as a councillor who listens, acts, and puts residents first.
